slides.title: Route Values Like a Pro
slides.body: Python's `match` statement looks at a value and **runs the code for the pattern that fits** — like a coffee shop routing each order to the right station. It's a cleaner alternative to long `if/elif` chains.
slides.tip: match requires Python 3.10 or newer — run `python --version` to check.

slides.title: Your First match Statement
slides.body: Write `match` followed by the value, then `case` branches for each pattern. Python checks **top to bottom** and stops at the first match.
slides.code:
day = "Friday"

match day:
    case "Monday":
        print("Start of the work week!")
    case "Friday":
        print("Almost the weekend!")
    case _:
        print("Just another day.")

slides.title: _ is the Catch-All Wildcard
slides.body: `case _:` matches **anything** that didn't match above — exactly like `else` in an `if` chain. Always put it last as your safety net.
slides.code:
code = 404

match code:
    case 200:
        print("OK")
    case 404:
        print("Not Found")
    case _:
        print(f"Unknown: {code}")
slides.tip: No break needed — Python never falls through from one case to the next.

slides.title: Match Multiple Values with |
slides.body: Use the pipe `|` inside a `case` to group values that share the same outcome. It means **"or"** — one branch handles them all.
slides.code:
grade = "B"

match grade:
    case "A":
        print("Excellent!")
    case "B" | "C":
        print("Good work, keep it up!")
    case _:
        print("Keep trying!")

slides.title: Match Structures Like Tuples
slides.body: `match` really shines with **structured values**. You can match on shape and content at once — variables in a pattern *capture* the value, not compare it.
slides.code:
point = (0, 5)

match point:
    case (0, 0):
        print("Origin")
    case (0, y):
        print(f"Y-axis at {y}")
    case (x, y):
        print(f"Point ({x}, {y})")

slides.title: You've Got match Mastered!
slides.body: `match` makes multi-branch logic **readable and expressive**. Use `case` for each pattern, `|` to combine values, `_` as your default, and unpack tuples right inside the pattern.
slides.tip: When you have more than 3-4 if/elif branches checking the same variable, match is usually the cleaner choice.
